heroOverview: A urethrocele is a downward prolapse of the urethra into the anterior vaginal wall, occurring when the connective tissue and fascia supporting the urethrovaginal septum weaken or stretch beyond their ability to maintain the urethra in its normal position. It almost exclusively affects women and frequently coexists with cystocele (bladder prolapse), forming part of the broader spectrum of anterior vaginal wall prolapse. Urethrocele is most often a consequence of childbirth-related pelvic floor damage, hormonal changes after menopause, or chronic elevated intra-abdominal pressure. The most prominent symptom is stress urinary incontinence — leakage of urine with coughing, sneezing, or exertion — though many women also report pelvic pressure, a bulge in the vagina, and voiding difficulty. Pelvic floor physiotherapy is the cornerstone of conservative management; surgical repair is reserved for cases that do not respond adequately.

keyStats.prevalence: Anterior vaginal wall prolapse (encompassing urethrocele and cystocele) affects approximately 10–30% of women who have had a vaginal delivery; urethrocele alone is less frequently isolated from the broader cystourethrocele syndrome
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects women; highest prevalence in parous postmenopausal women
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed in women aged 50–70, though symptoms can emerge in the 30s and 40s after multiple vaginal deliveries
keyStats.globalCases: Pelvic organ prolapse — of which urethrocele is a subtype — affects an estimated 40% of parous women worldwide based on clinical examination in population studies

definition: A urethrocele (ICD-10: N81.0) is a form of anterior vaginal wall prolapse in which the urethra descends into the vaginal lumen due to laxity of the pubourethral ligaments, periurethral fascia, and the anterior vaginal wall that normally keep the urethra supported against the pubic symphysis. It is distinguished from a cystocele (bladder prolapse) by its location at the lowest portion of the anterior vaginal wall — at the vaginal introitus — rather than higher up in the vaginal vault. In practice, the two frequently occur together as a cystourethrocele. The pathophysiology centres on weakening of the fascial and ligamentous supports of the urethra and bladder neck, which normally maintain the proximal urethra above the pelvic floor, allowing urethral closure pressure to exceed bladder pressure during increases in abdominal pressure. When this support fails, the urethrovesical junction descends, its closure mechanism is compromised, and stress urinary incontinence results. Causes include vaginal delivery (particularly prolonged second stage, large babies, and instrumental delivery), oestrogen deficiency after menopause, connective tissue disorders, chronic constipation, obesity, and repetitive heavy lifting. Urethrocele is classified according to the Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q) staging system (stages I–IV) based on the extent of descent. It falls under urology and urogynaecology, a subspecialty addressing both structural prolapse and related bladder dysfunction.

typesClassification.description: Prolapse of the urethra only into the anterior vaginal wall, most often at stages I or II of the POP-Q system; produces mainly stress incontinence and urethral discomfort without a large anterior vaginal bulge.
Combined prolapse of the bladder and urethra into the anterior vaginal wall, the most common form in clinical practice; produces stress incontinence, pelvic pressure, and voiding difficulty together.
Urethrocele coexisting with rectocele or uterine prolapse; common in older parous women and usually requires combined surgical repair.
Urethral descent to or beyond the vaginal introitus; produces a visible or palpable vaginal bulge and significant voiding and continence symptoms.

primarySymptoms: Stress urinary incontinence — involuntary leakage of urine during activities that raise intra-abdominal pressure, such as coughing, sneezing, laughing, lifting, or exercising.
A sensation of pelvic pressure, heaviness, or dragging in the lower pelvis, particularly after prolonged standing or physical activity.
A palpable or visible bulge at the vaginal opening, felt by the patient when wiping or during bathing, representing the prolapsed urethral and anterior vaginal tissue.
Urinary urgency and frequency, resulting from secondary bladder overactivity caused by the altered anatomy of the bladder neck and urethra.
Difficulty starting or maintaining the urinary stream (voiding dysfunction), particularly in higher-grade prolapse when the urethra becomes kinked.
Incomplete bladder emptying and a post-void sensation of residual urine, increasing the risk of urinary tract infections.
Discomfort or pain during sexual intercourse (dyspareunia) related to the prolapsed tissue at the vaginal introitus.
Urethral discharge or bleeding from erosion of the prolapsed tissue in advanced cases, particularly when the mucosa becomes exposed and traumatised.
Recurrent urinary tract infections, resulting from incomplete bladder emptying and colonisation of residual urine.
Backache or sacral discomfort, reflecting altered pelvic statics caused by the weakened pelvic floor support.
earlyWarningSigns: Occasional urine leakage with vigorous exercise or laughing that the woman initially attributes to ageing or postnatal changes.
A mild sensation of vaginal heaviness at the end of the day that disappears on lying down.
Needing to press fingers against the anterior vaginal wall to complete urination — a manoeuvre that digitally reduces the prolapse and restores normal voiding.
Increased frequency of urinary tract infections without an obvious infectious cause.
emergencySigns: Complete urinary retention — inability to pass any urine — particularly in high-grade prolapse where the urethra is acutely kinked and obstructed; requires emergency catheterisation.
A large, dark-red or ulcerated vaginal bulge suggesting advanced prolapse with mucosal necrosis, requiring urgent urogynaecological assessment.
Acute onset of severe pelvic pain with a recently prolapsed structure, suggesting incarceration.
Signs of urosepsis — high fever, rigors, confusion — in a woman with known urethrocele and recent urinary tract infection.

causes.description: Vaginal delivery is the single most significant cause of urethrocele. Stretching of the pubourethral ligaments, tearing of the periurethral fascia, and pudendal nerve damage during prolonged second-stage labour, large-for-gestational-age delivery, or instrumental (forceps or vacuum) delivery permanently weaken urethral support.

Falling oestrogen levels cause atrophy of the urogenital mucosa, thinning of the vaginal wall, and loss of collagen from the pubourethral ligaments and periurethral fascia. This accelerates the descent of already weakened tissues and can cause previously asymptomatic prolapse to become symptomatic.

Prolonged straining at stool from chronic constipation, heavy lifting, chronic cough from smoking or pulmonary disease, and obesity all transmit raised pressure to the pelvic floor over time, progressively stretching and fatiguing the supporting structures.

Inherited conditions including Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and Marfan syndrome reduce collagen quality and quantity, causing ligamentous laxity that predisposes to pelvic organ prolapse at a younger age, often without the usual history of multiple vaginal deliveries.

Removal of the uterus eliminates a key midline support structure. Even with careful surgical technique, hysterectomy can disturb the lateral and apical support of the anterior vaginal wall, predisposing to later urethrocele or cystocele development.

causes.cause: Obesity and high body mass index
causes.description: Excess abdominal weight chronically elevates intra-abdominal pressure and thereby continuously loads the pelvic floor, accelerating the stretching of fascial and ligamentous supports throughout adult life.

riskFactors.description: Each vaginal delivery increases urethrocele risk; women with three or more vaginal deliveries have approximately 3–4 times the risk compared with nulliparous women. Caesarean delivery is protective but does not eliminate all risk.
Loss of oestrogen accelerates connective tissue and mucosal thinning in the anterior vaginal wall; topical vaginal oestrogen can partly mitigate this risk in the postmenopausal period.
BMI above 30 kg/m² significantly increases pelvic floor load; weight reduction of 5–10% of body weight measurably reduces prolapse symptoms and stress incontinence severity.
Habitual straining at stool generates repeated sharp elevations in intra-abdominal pressure; treating constipation with dietary fibre, hydration, and laxatives is a modifiable prevention target.
Occupations requiring regular heavy lifting chronically load the pelvic floor; ergonomic modifications and pelvic floor strengthening help offset the risk.
Heritable connective tissue conditions reduce collagen quality; affected women may develop significant prolapse after even one vaginal delivery or in the absence of pregnancy.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of pelvic organ prolapse
riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with prolapse increases personal risk approximately twofold to threefold, reflecting shared genetic variation in collagen metabolism and pelvic floor anatomy.
Hysterectomy, anti-incontinence surgery, and bladder procedures alter pelvic floor mechanics and can predispose to new or recurrent prolapse.

affectedDemographics: Parous women (those who have given birth), particularly after vaginal delivery
Postmenopausal women in whom oestrogen deficiency accelerates tissue atrophy
Women with obesity or chronic constipation
Women with connective tissue disorders or a strong family history of prolapse
Women who have had a hysterectomy or other pelvic surgery
Older women aged 50–70, in whom cumulative tissue weakening becomes symptomatic
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is made clinically through history and pelvic examination. The woman is asked to strain or cough (Valsalva manoeuvre) while the clinician inspects the vaginal introitus with a Sim's speculum, identifying descent of the anterior vaginal wall. The POP-Q staging system quantifies the most advanced point of prolapse relative to the hymen, providing a standardised severity score from stage I (above the hymen) to stage IV (complete procidentia). A half-speculum is used to retract the posterior wall, allowing isolated visualisation of the anterior compartment. Concurrent uterine prolapse, cystocele, rectocele, and enterocele are assessed at the same examination. Urodynamic testing — including cystometry (bladder pressure during filling) and urethral pressure profilometry — is performed before surgery in most cases to characterise the type and severity of incontinence and detect occult detrusor overactivity that might not be apparent on history alone. Post-void residual urine measurement by bladder ultrasound quantifies incomplete bladder emptying. Renal tract ultrasound is added when upper tract obstruction is suspected in high-grade bilateral hydronephrosis. Cystoscopy is performed when haematuria is present to exclude bladder pathology. Key differential diagnoses are isolated cystocele, urethral diverticulum, anterior vaginal wall cyst, and, rarely, a urethral caruncle or carcinoma.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical pelvic examination and POP-Q staging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and grades the degree of urethral descent using the validated Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification system, guiding treatment decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie on your back with knees bent; the clinician inserts a speculum and asks you to bear down. The examination takes about 5–10 minutes and may cause mild discomfort. POP-Q measurements are taken using a ruler.

diagnosticTests.test: Urodynamic studies (cystometry and urethral pressure profile)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the type of urinary incontinence (stress, urgency, or mixed), quantifies urethral closure pressure, and detects detrusor overactivity before surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small catheter is inserted into the bladder and sometimes the rectum; the bladder is filled slowly with water while pressure is measured. The test takes 30–60 minutes, involves voiding at the end, and produces some mild bladder discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the volume of urine remaining in the bladder after voiding to detect incomplete emptying caused by urethral kinking in high-grade prolapse.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is placed on your lower abdomen immediately after you urinate; painless, takes 2–3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Records the rate and pattern of urine flow to detect obstructed voiding related to urethral descent or kinking in high-grade urethrocele.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You urinate into a funnel-shaped container connected to a flow meter; the procedure takes as long as your voiding takes and is completely non-invasive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out upper urinary tract obstruction and hydronephrosis when high-grade prolapse causes suspected bilateral ureteral kinking or when renal function is impaired.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An abdominal ultrasound probe is gently pressed over the flanks; painless, takes 15–20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visual inspection of the bladder and urethra, performed when haematuria, recurrent infection, or suspected bladder pathology needs to be excluded before surgical repair.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible or rigid telescope is passed through the urethra under local anaesthetic gel; there is some stinging during insertion. The procedure takes 5–10 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is matched to symptom severity, grade of prolapse, the patient's reproductive plans, age, fitness, and preference. Conservative management is the first-line approach for all but the most severe cases. Supervised pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) — a structured programme of Kegel exercises taught by a pelvic floor physiotherapist — is the most evidence-based non-surgical treatment: meta-analyses show it reduces stress incontinence episodes by approximately 50–70% and can improve mild-to-moderate prolapse symptoms. Vaginal pessaries — ring, Gellhorn, or dish devices inserted into the vagina — mechanically support the prolapsed tissue and provide effective long-term symptom relief without surgery, with satisfaction rates of 60–85% in women who retain them at one year. Topical vaginal oestrogen in postmenopausal women improves tissue quality, aids pessary retention, and reduces UTI risk. When conservative measures are inadequate, surgery is considered. The standard surgical repair for urethrocele is anterior colporrhaphy — plication of the pubocervical fascia under the anterior vaginal wall through a vaginal incision — often combined with a mid-urethral sling procedure (TVT or TOT) if stress incontinence is a major component. Mesh-augmented repairs offer better anatomical durability in selected cases but carry mesh-specific complications, including erosion and pain, and their use is regulated differently across health systems. Women with concurrent uterine prolapse, cystocele, or rectocele often require combined repair at the same operation. Recurrence rates after anterior colporrhaphy are approximately 20–30% at ten years; augmented repairs have lower anatomical recurrence but higher complication rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT / Kegel exercises)
medicalTreatments.description: A structured programme of repeated voluntary contractions of the levator ani and periurethral muscles, typically supervised by a pelvic floor physiotherapist with at least three sessions over three months. The goal is to increase resting urethral closure pressure and improve the active elevation of the bladder neck during sudden rises in abdominal pressure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show a 50–70% reduction in stress incontinence episodes and clinically significant improvement in prolapse symptoms in 60–70% of women who adhere to supervised programmes over three months.

medicalTreatments.name: Vaginal pessary
medicalTreatments.description: A silicone device inserted into the vagina to mechanically support the prolapsed urethra and anterior vaginal wall. Ring pessaries are used for mild-to-moderate cases; Gellhorn or cube pessaries for higher-grade prolapse. The pessary is fitted in clinic and replaced every three to six months. Topical oestrogen improves comfort and reduces erosion risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 60–85% of women who are fitted retain and are satisfied with a pessary at one year; long-term use is successful in many women who are not surgical candidates.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical vaginal oestrogen
medicalTreatments.description: Applied as cream, pessary, or ring directly to the vaginal mucosa in postmenopausal women to restore tissue thickness and elasticity of the periurethral fascia and vaginal wall. Reduces UTI frequency and improves pessary tolerance. Not appropriate for women with oestrogen-sensitive tumours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces symptomatic vaginal atrophy in approximately 80–90% of postmenopausal women; modestly reduces prolapse symptom severity and recurrent UTI frequency.

medicalTreatments.name: Bladder training and lifestyle modification
medicalTreatments.description: Timed voiding intervals gradually increased over weeks to reduce urgency and frequency; combined with fluid management, caffeine reduction, and weight loss. Addresses bladder overactivity that frequently coexists with urethrocele and stress incontinence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bladder training reduces urgency incontinence episodes by 50–80% in studies of women with mixed or urgency-predominant incontinence.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral medication that increases urethral sphincter tone by enhancing motor neuron activity at the pudendal nucleus; approved for moderate-to-severe stress incontinence as a pharmacological bridge to surgery or in women who decline surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces stress incontinence episode frequency by approximately 50–64% compared with placebo in randomised trials; benefit must be weighed against side effects including nausea in approximately 20% of users.

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior colporrhaphy (anterior repair)
surgicalOptions.description: The primary surgical repair for urethrocele and anterior vaginal wall prolapse. Through a vaginal incision in the anterior wall, the pubocervical fascia is plicated (folded and sutured) under the urethra and bladder, reinforcing the support layer. Often performed alongside a mid-urethral sling for concurrent stress incontinence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Subjective success rate approximately 70–80% at five years; anatomical recurrence at 10 years is 20–30%, reflecting the need for durable fascial repair.

surgicalOptions.name: Mid-urethral sling (TVT or TOT)
surgicalOptions.description: A polypropylene tape is placed under the mid-urethra through vaginal and small abdominal or inner-thigh incisions, restoring the suburethral support lost with urethrocele. Performed as a standalone procedure for stress incontinence or combined with anterior colporrhaphy for concurrent prolapse.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure or significant improvement of stress urinary incontinence in approximately 80–85% of women at five years; one of the most studied surgical procedures for female stress incontinence.

surgicalOptions.name: Colposuspension (Burch procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: An open or laparoscopic procedure in which the bladder neck and anterior vaginal wall are suspended to the iliopectineal ligament, elevating the urethra. Less commonly performed than mid-urethral sling but remains an option, particularly at the time of abdominal surgery for concurrent prolapse.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term continence rates of approximately 70–80% at 10–20 years; slightly lower than sling procedures but avoids mesh under the urethra.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and biofeedback
alternativeTreatments.description: Some women report modest improvement in urge incontinence with acupuncture or EMG biofeedback-assisted pelvic floor training; evidence quality is moderate and these are used as adjuncts to PFMT rather than primary treatments.

alternativeTreatments.name: Weight loss programme
alternativeTreatments.description: A 5–10% reduction in body weight reduces stress incontinence episodes by approximately 40–50% in overweight and obese women — an evidence-based non-surgical intervention that also improves general health.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelvic floor support garments
alternativeTreatments.description: Compression shorts or pelvic support garments provide external mechanical support and reduce stress leakage during exercise; used as a symptom management tool while PFMT or surgical treatment is being arranged.

whySeeSpecialist: Urogynaecologists and urogynecological urologists manage the intersection of pelvic floor structural failure and bladder dysfunction. A specialist should be seen when symptoms significantly affect quality of life, when conservative treatment has failed, when voiding difficulty suggests urethral obstruction requiring urodynamic assessment, when surgery is being considered, or when the prolapse is grade III–IV. Women with recurrent prolapse after prior surgery require specialist assessment to plan optimal re-operative technique.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified urogynaecologist or a urologist with a subspecialty in female pelvic medicine and reconstructive surgery (FPMRS). Access to in-house urodynamic testing, pelvic floor physiotherapy, and surgical expertise in both native-tissue and mesh-augmented repair is important. Ask how many anterior repairs and mid-urethral slings the surgeon performs annually — volume is associated with lower complication and recurrence rates. Pelvic floor physiotherapy should be offered as first-line before surgery is discussed.
preventionStrategies: Begin supervised pelvic floor exercises (Kegel exercises) during pregnancy and resume them promptly after vaginal delivery to strengthen urethral support.
Maintain a healthy body weight; BMI above 30 significantly increases prolapse risk and stress incontinence severity.
Treat chronic constipation with dietary fibre, adequate hydration, and laxatives to eliminate habitual straining.
Use topical vaginal oestrogen after menopause to preserve tissue quality and periurethral fascial strength.
Avoid heavy prolonged lifting without proper breath support and pelvic floor activation (correct lifting technique).
Seek prompt surgical consultation for obstetric tears and pelvic floor injuries after delivery, which if repaired early reduce late prolapse risk.
lifestyleModifications: Practise pelvic floor exercises at least three times daily, performing 10–15 sustained contractions of three to eight seconds each.
Reduce caffeine and alcohol intake, which increase bladder irritability and worsen urgency symptoms that coexist with urethrocele.
Adopt timed voiding (every 2–3 hours) and avoid habitually voiding 'just in case', which trains the bladder to hold smaller volumes.
Use a raised toilet seat or foot stool that supports a forward-lean voiding posture, improving bladder emptying in prolapse.
Wear an incontinence pad only as a short-term measure while seeking treatment — do not use pads indefinitely as the primary management strategy.

recommended: High-fibre foods (vegetables, fruits, wholegrains, legumes) to prevent constipation and reduce straining
At least 1.5–2 litres of fluid daily to keep urine dilute and reduce bladder irritability
Maintaining a calorie balance that supports achieving and sustaining a healthy body weight
avoid: Excess caffeine (coffee, tea, cola) and alcohol, both of which irritate the bladder and worsen urgency incontinence
Highly acidic or spicy foods if they worsen urgency symptoms in your individual case
Excessive fluid restriction, which concentrates urine and paradoxically worsens urgency

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic exercise such as walking, swimming, and cycling is encouraged. High-impact activities — running, jumping, competitive sport — should be approached with caution and a pelvic floor physiotherapist consulted about how to modify technique and use a pessary or support garment during exercise. Avoid prolonged heavy resistance exercises such as heavy barbell squatting or deadlifting until the pelvic floor is sufficiently strong. After surgical repair, most women return to light activity at six weeks and full activity at three months.
dailyManagement: Keep a bladder diary for three days to identify patterns of leakage, urgency, and voiding frequency before appointments.
Insert and care for a pessary as instructed; return for scheduled changes and never wear a pessary beyond its advised review interval.
Practise Kegel exercises daily as prescribed; use a phone reminder to build the habit.
Monitor for new urinary tract infection symptoms (burning, frequency, fever) as incomplete emptying raises UTI risk.
Report any vaginal bleeding, new onset of pain, or significant change in symptoms promptly.
prognosis: The prognosis for urethrocele is positive when treatment is tailored to severity. Mild-to-moderate urethrocele managed with pelvic floor physiotherapy and a vaginal pessary achieves satisfactory symptom control in the majority of women over the long term, without surgery. Surgical anterior colporrhaphy restores normal anatomy and improves quality of life in 70–80% of women at five years, though anatomical recurrence at 10 years reaches 20–30%. Addition of a mid-urethral sling improves continence outcomes significantly when stress incontinence is the main concern. Women who have surgery before progressive pelvic floor damage accumulates have better durability of repair. Urethrocele itself does not carry risks of malignant transformation, but the associated symptoms — incontinence, incomplete emptying, and recurrent UTIs — can substantially affect quality of life, making active management important.
recoveryTimeline: After conservative treatment, improvement in stress incontinence from PFMT is typically apparent at six to twelve weeks of supervised exercises. Pessary fitting provides immediate mechanical relief. After anterior colporrhaphy, hospital stay is one to two days. Return to light daily activities occurs within two to four weeks; return to driving at two to three weeks; return to strenuous exercise or sexual intercourse at six to eight weeks. Full surgical healing and fibrosis of the repair takes three to six months, during which time lifting over five kilograms is avoided.
complications: Stress urinary incontinence — the most common functional consequence; leakage during coughing, sneezing, and exercise affects quality of life and requires active treatment.
Recurrent urinary tract infections — incomplete bladder emptying provides a reservoir for bacterial growth, increasing UTI frequency in women with significant urethrocele.
Voiding difficulty and urinary retention — high-grade urethrocele kinked the urethra causing obstructive voiding; acute retention requires emergency catheterisation.
Prolapse progression — untreated or inadequately treated urethrocele can worsen over time, particularly with ongoing risk factors such as constipation or obesity.

Post-surgical complications — anterior colporrhaphy and mid-urethral slings carry risks of de novo urgency incontinence, voiding difficulty, mesh erosion (sling-specific), wound haematoma, and urinary tract injury.
De novo urgency incontinence — correcting the anatomical prolapse surgically can unmask or worsen bladder overactivity in 10–15% of women, sometimes exchanging one symptom for another.

supportResources.name: Pelvic Floor First (Continence Foundation of Australia)
supportResources.description: Evidence-based resource on pelvic floor health, prolapse, and incontinence with exercises and practitioner finder.

supportResources.name: NICE NG123 — Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse in women
supportResources.description: NICE clinical guideline providing evidence-based recommendations for treating urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse, including urethrocele.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cystocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A cystocele is prolapse of the bladder through the upper anterior vaginal wall; a urethrocele involves the urethra at the lower anterior vaginal wall. They are anatomically distinct but frequently coexist, and isolated urethrocele alone is less common.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urethral diverticulum
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A urethral diverticulum is an outpouching of the urethral wall forming a cystic sac, producing post-void dribbling and a midline anterior vaginal wall mass; it does not descend into the vagina with Valsalva as a urethrocele does.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stress urinary incontinence
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stress urinary incontinence is the functional symptom of urine leakage with exertion; urethrocele is the structural anatomical diagnosis — the prolapse — that frequently causes stress incontinence but not in all cases, and incontinence can occur without significant anatomical prolapse.

relatedConditions.name: Cystocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Cystocele (bladder prolapse) is the most closely related condition, sharing the same anatomical compartment, risk factors, and treatment pathway; the two coexist as cystourethrocele in the majority of clinical cases.

relatedConditions.name: Stress urinary incontinence
relatedConditions.relevance: Stress incontinence is the primary functional consequence of urethrocele; its assessment drives treatment decisions and surgical planning.

relatedConditions.name: Uterovaginal prolapse
relatedConditions.relevance: Uterovaginal prolapse frequently coexists with anterior compartment prolapse (urethrocele and cystocele) as part of global pelvic floor weakness, often requiring combined surgical repair.

relatedConditions.name: Overactive bladder
relatedConditions.relevance: Overactive bladder and urgency incontinence frequently coexist with urethrocele; urodynamic assessment distinguishes the relative contribution of each to the patient's symptoms before treatment is planned.

faqs.question: What is a urethrocele?
faqs.answer: A urethrocele is a downward prolapse of the urethra — the tube that carries urine out of the body — into the anterior vaginal wall. It occurs when the ligaments and fascia supporting the urethra weaken, most often from childbirth, menopause, or chronic straining. It almost exclusively affects women.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a urethrocele and a cystocele?
faqs.answer: A urethrocele is prolapse of the urethra at the lower part of the anterior vaginal wall; a cystocele is prolapse of the bladder through the upper anterior vaginal wall. They share the same risk factors and often occur together. A urethrocele mainly causes stress incontinence; a cystocele also causes pelvic pressure and voiding difficulty.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a urethrocele?
faqs.answer: The most common symptom is stress urinary incontinence — leaking urine when coughing, sneezing, laughing, or exercising. Other symptoms include a sensation of pelvic heaviness or a vaginal bulge, urinary urgency and frequency, difficulty fully emptying the bladder, and discomfort during intercourse.

faqs.question: What causes a urethrocele?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is vaginal childbirth, particularly difficult or instrumental deliveries that damage the ligaments supporting the urethra. Menopause-related oestrogen decline, obesity, chronic constipation, heavy lifting, and connective tissue disorders are other important causes.

faqs.question: Can a urethrocele be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises) with a physiotherapist reduces stress incontinence by 50–70% in many women. A vaginal pessary mechanically supports the prolapse and provides effective long-term symptom control for women who prefer to avoid surgery. Topical vaginal oestrogen helps after menopause.

faqs.question: How are Kegel exercises done?
faqs.answer: Contract the muscles you would use to stop the flow of urine. Hold the contraction for 3–8 seconds, then fully relax for the same time. Aim for 10–15 repetitions per set, three sets daily. It takes 6–12 weeks of consistent practice to notice a meaningful improvement in stress incontinence symptoms.

faqs.question: What surgery is used for a urethrocele?
faqs.answer: Anterior colporrhaphy is the standard operation, in which the pubocervical fascia under the urethra is tightened through a vaginal incision. If stress incontinence is significant, a mid-urethral sling (TVT or TOT) is usually performed at the same time. Surgery is reserved for cases where conservative treatment has not provided adequate relief.

faqs.question: Does a urethrocele cause urinary incontinence?
faqs.answer: Yes. A urethrocele is one of the main structural causes of stress urinary incontinence. When the urethra descends, its closing mechanism is compromised, allowing urine to leak during sudden increases in abdominal pressure such as coughing, sneezing, or exercise.

faqs.question: Can a urethrocele come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anatomical recurrence after anterior colporrhaphy occurs in approximately 20–30% of women at 10 years. Recurrence is more common in women with ongoing risk factors such as obesity, constipation, or connective tissue disorders. Pelvic floor exercises after surgery help maintain the repair.

faqs.question: Is a urethrocele dangerous?
faqs.answer: A urethrocele is not life-threatening but can significantly affect quality of life through incontinence, recurrent UTIs, and difficulty emptying the bladder. High-grade prolapse causing urinary retention is a complication requiring prompt treatment. Urethrocele itself carries no cancer risk.

faqs.question: What does a urethrocele feel like?
faqs.answer: Women often describe a sensation of pelvic heaviness or dragging, particularly after standing for long periods, and may feel or see a soft bulge at the vaginal opening. Some report having to press on the front wall of the vagina to finish urinating. The condition may cause no noticeable sensation at lower grades.

faqs.question: How is a urethrocele diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A clinician diagnoses urethrocele by examining the vagina while you bear down (Valsalva manoeuvre), using a Sim's speculum to see the anterior vaginal wall descent. The POP-Q system grades severity. Urodynamic tests measure bladder and urethral pressures, and bladder ultrasound assesses residual urine.

faqs.question: Can childbirth cause a urethrocele?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vaginal delivery, particularly prolonged labour, a large baby, or forceps or vacuum delivery, stretches and may tear the ligaments and fascia that hold the urethra in place. The damage may not cause symptoms until later in life, when additional factors such as menopause accelerate tissue weakening.

faqs.question: Does a urethrocele affect sexual function?
faqs.answer: A urethrocele can cause dyspareunia — discomfort during intercourse — due to the prolapsed tissue at the vaginal introitus. Some women avoid sexual activity because of incontinence during intercourse. After successful treatment, sexual function typically improves, and incontinence during intercourse often resolves.

faqs.question: Can losing weight help a urethrocele?
faqs.answer: Yes. A 5–10% reduction in body weight reduces the frequency of stress incontinence episodes by approximately 40–50% in overweight women. Weight loss reduces the chronic abdominal pressure on the pelvic floor and is recommended as a first-line lifestyle intervention before or alongside other treatments.

faqs.question: Can a urethrocele cause recurrent urinary tract infections?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the prolapse prevents the bladder from emptying completely, stagnant residual urine becomes a site for bacterial growth. Women with urethrocele and repeated UTIs should have their post-void residual measured; treating the prolapse reduces UTI frequency by restoring more complete bladder emptying.

faqs.question: What is a vaginal pessary for urethrocele?
faqs.answer: A pessary is a silicone device inserted into the vagina that supports the prolapsed tissue mechanically. It is fitted by a clinician, with ring pessaries used for mild-to-moderate cases. It is removed, cleaned, and replaced every three to six months. About 60–85% of women are satisfied with a pessary at one year.

faqs.question: Can a urethrocele be prevented?
faqs.answer: Pelvic floor exercises during and after pregnancy, maintaining a healthy weight, treating constipation, avoiding prolonged heavy lifting, and using topical vaginal oestrogen after menopause all reduce the risk or slow progression. Some risk is non-modifiable, including genetic factors and the number of vaginal deliveries.

faqs.question: Is a urethrocele the same as pelvic organ prolapse?
faqs.answer: A urethrocele is one type of pelvic organ prolapse — specifically, prolapse of the urethra. Pelvic organ prolapse is the broader category that includes cystocele (bladder), rectocele (rectum), uterine prolapse, and vaginal vault prolapse. Many women have more than one type simultaneously.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery if I have a urethrocele but no symptoms?
faqs.answer: No. Asymptomatic urethrocele does not require surgery. Conservative measures to prevent progression — pelvic floor exercises, weight management, constipation treatment — are appropriate. Surgery is considered only when symptoms significantly affect quality of life or when complications such as voiding difficulty or recurrent UTI develop.

metaDescription: A urethrocele is urethral prolapse into the vaginal wall, causing stress incontinence and pelvic pressure. Learn causes, Kegel exercises and surgical repair.
